feedback.text: I am quite concerned regarding the current state of unfair and frankly hostile anti-consumer practices in the market of digital goods and services. Since this is a newer market, and this frequently occurs in the context of video games, I am under the impression that this flies under the radar far more often than 'real world' equivalents. So let me illustrate by comparing this to movies. Imagine if movie makers were able to unilaterally decide to remotely stop their movie from playing ever again, including all copies sold to consumers who rightly expected their copy of the work to remain functional indefinitely, thereby effectively destroying the work forever. That's what's happening in the digital market right now. Moreover, we are faced with initiatives such as Collective Shout who, by their own admission, are pressuring digital payment processors to impose their personal values on what can and cannot be sold. Thereby applying a dangerous level of censorship that should not be in the hands of lobbyists, nor in the hands of the small handful of companies that serve as primary intermediaries between sellers and buyers. In the digital as well as the physical world, it should be everyone's individual choice what to sell, what to buy, and what to do with their rightful purchases - within the legal boundaries of global and local laws, naturally. It should not be possible to supersede the law by restricting anyone's ability in that regard, nor to effectively take away a past purchase by 'turning off' the product at the whims of its creator, without so much as warning given at the time of purchase. Finally, I would like to call attention to calls from governmental bodies to effectively require citizens to give up their right to privacy in order to access certain spaces on the internet, going against the fundamental precepts of Internet Freedom and the very cornerstones of privacy protection as laid out by the EU. It is my sincere hope that you will consider legislation that will protect citizens from any of these stated instances of censorship, loss of privacy rights, loss of access to the ability to purchase digital goods and services, and indeed what could be considered actual theft of previous purchases. We need legal recourse against these practices, and I hope we can count on you to provide us with options to protect our rights from bad actors seeking to usurp control of what is an important part of many people's livelihoods. Thank you for your time.
